questions.question: What is a key difference between hypomania and full mania?

options.text: Hypomania lasts longer than full mania
Full mania causes severe impairment in functioning, may include psychosis, and often requires hospitalization, while hypomania does not severely impair functioning
Hypomania is always pleasant while mania is always unpleasant
There is no meaningful difference between the two

questions.explanation: Full mania causes severe impairment in functioning, may include psychotic features (delusions, hallucinations), and often requires hospitalization. Hypomania involves a noticeable change from baseline but does not cause the same level of functional impairment and never includes psychosis. Both involve elevated or irritable mood, but the severity and consequences differ significantly.

questions.question: Why is hypomania considered dangerous despite feeling good?

options.text: Because it always causes physical pain
Because feeling good makes people less likely to recognize it as a symptom, leading to treatment resistance, medication discontinuation, and potential escalation to full mania
Because it only occurs in people with Bipolar I disorder
Because it never leads to any real consequences

questions.explanation: Hypomania is dangerous precisely because it feels good. People may not recognize it as a symptom ('This is just who I really am'), resist treatment ('Why would I want to get rid of this?'), stop medication ('I feel great, I don't need it'), or miss that it is escalating toward full mania. The choices made during hypomania often have consequences the stable self must deal with.

questions.question: What is the '72-hour rule' for managing elevated mood states?

options.text: Wait 72 hours before taking any medication during a manic episode
Exercise intensely for 72 hours to burn off excess energy
Before making any major decision during elevated mood, wait at least 72 hours and consult at least one trusted person
Stay awake for 72 hours to test whether the elevated mood passes on its own

questions.explanation: The 72-hour rule means that before making any major decision (financial, relationship, career, or life changes) during a period of elevated mood, you wait at least 72 hours and consult a trusted person. This allows the initial surge of certainty to moderate, enables objective input, and recognizes that most 'urgent' elevated-mood ideas are not actually time-sensitive. This rule should be established during a stable period.

questions.question: What is the single most important behavioral intervention during an escalating elevated mood episode?

options.text: Intense physical exercise to channel the energy productively
Increasing social engagement to get external feedback
Protecting sleep -- maintaining a consistent bedtime and contacting your treatment team if sleep drops below 5 hours for two or more nights
Starting new creative projects to take advantage of the energy

questions.explanation: Sleep protection is the single most important behavioral intervention during elevated mood. Sleep disruption is both a symptom and a cause of escalating mania. Maintaining a consistent bedtime, creating a calm sleep environment, and contacting your treatment team if sleep drops below 5 hours for two or more nights are critical steps. Intense exercise and new projects can actually fuel escalation.

questions.question: Why is it advised NOT to 'ride the wave' of hypomanic productivity?

options.text: Because the work produced during hypomania is always low quality
Because using the burst of energy feeds the escalation and often leads to a depressive crash -- the energy is borrowed from your stability
Because it is illegal to work during a hypomanic episode
Because you should only work when you feel depressed

questions.explanation: Riding the wave of hypomanic productivity -- using the burst of energy to accomplish tasks, create, or socialize -- feeds the mood escalation. The energy being spent is borrowed from mood stability, and what goes up will come down, often into a depressive crash. The responsible approach is to implement stimulus control and slow down, even though it feels counterintuitive.
